fields.backstory:
Lillith was born beneath Halsperia, in the Undershadows of the dry east, as a daughter of House Baerne, a powerful drow house whose name carried weight even in places where it was never spoken aloud. Her mother, Matron Vendes Baerne, ruled with the kind of authority that did not need to raise its voice. Within their halls, men were tools, daughters were weapons, and affection was only tolerated when it could be turned into leverage.

House Baerne survived through patience, bloodline, trade, and the careful tightening of invisible threads. Their influence reached further than the Undershadows through an old alliance with an Anansi tribe whose hierarchy mirrored their own: matriarchs at the centre, family arranged like a web, loyalty measured by usefulness, and every promise tied to something that could later be pulled. Through this alliance, House Baerne held routes, rumours, debts, and access to Halsperia that other houses could not easily touch.

Lillith was not raised as a spare daughter. She was watched. Measured. Sharpened. From a young age, she was taught that survival belonged to those willing to strike first. She learned courtly cruelty, silent observation, poisoned politeness, and the value of a bargain that left the other person smiling while bleeding. She was not poor at these lessons. That was the problem. Her mother saw promise in her and began preparing her for a higher role within the house.

For a time, Lillith believed this was strength.

The breaking point did not come all at once. It came in small lessons, each one more rotten than the last. She was asked to smile while alliances were broken. To watch men kneel for forgiveness they would never receive. To stand silent while daughters were praised for becoming crueler than the women who raised them. She was taught to hurt what resisted, to punish what embarrassed the house, and to call it discipline.

Still, Lillith endured it.

Then her mother’s schemes required a sacrifice. A man loyal to the house, whether kin, guard, servant, or consort, was accused of treachery to cover a failure that belonged to someone far above him. His guilt did not matter. His usefulness had ended. Lillith was expected to stand beside her mother, affirm the lie, and prove that she understood what power demanded.

She was expected to take part in it.

For one long moment, Lillith said nothing. She swallowed her tongue until blood was drawn, because to speak against the Matron in that hall would have brought a volatile reaction no blade could easily survive. Lillith was aggressive, and at times somewhat unpredictable, but she was not stupid. She was observant enough, and strong enough, to know when rage would only make her another corpse on the floor.

So she watched an innocent man’s life end.

And in that silence, she saw the rot beneath it.

The cruelty of her people no longer looked like dominance. It looked like fear dressed in silk. Every alliance was temporary. Every family bond was a blade held close to the throat. Every victory only fed the next betrayal. Lillith did not suddenly become merciful, but she understood then that she did not admire them. She only knew how to survive them.

So she severed herself from the house.

She left without permission, without blessing, and without any illusion that she would be forgiven. House Baerne does not misplace its daughters. It counts them. It remembers them. One day, Lillith knows, someone will be sent to drag her name back into the dark, or cut it from her throat.

In the world above, her name and nature closed many doors, but Lillith had been raised among worse things than suspicion. She travelled through Halsperia, crossed seas, and eventually forged a route toward Aeternia, carrying goods, letters, secrets, and favours between people who had no reason to trust one another.

Over the years, she became a seasoned courier, trader, smuggler, and occasional adventurer. She learned to haggle her way out of bad bargains, sneer through insults, and keep unlikely allies alive just long enough for them to become useful again. The road gave her distance. The sea gave her silence.

It was there, far from the Undershadows, that Lillith found Thalassa.

Not as a gentle goddess. Not as salvation. But as a dead mother whose body became the world. A symbol of violent rebirth. A primordial force betrayed, broken, and remade into life itself. To Lillith, Thalassa was everything her own mother was not: vast instead of possessive, chaotic instead of controlled, generative even in death.

Lillith’s devotion began as defiance. If blood had chained her to one mother, faith would bind her to another by choice. Through Thalassa, she seeks to drown the inheritance of her house, bury the girl her mother raised, and be remade into something no matron, bloodline, or underworld throne can claim.

But her faith did not soften her. It gave her direction.

Lillith knows the people who raised her cannot simply be changed. Not by pleading. Not by reason. Not by one runaway daughter returning with cleaner hands and a kinder mouth. But as a cleric, she believes a story can travel further than a blade. Her past, her defiance, her devotion, and her refusal to be owned may one day reach the ears of her own kind. And when she meets them again, they may fight her to the death, or open their bloodied hearts to something new.

Whether she likes it or not, Lillith has leadership within her. Not the leadership her mother wanted. Not command built on fear, inheritance, and obedience. Something louder. Stranger. Chosen.

She does not know whether Thalassa offers forgiveness.

But she believes the sea knows how to swallow a corpse.
fields.personalityIdealsBonds:
Lillith serves her own interest first, but she is not foolish enough to waste a useful alliance. She was raised in a world where survival belonged to the ruthless, and that instinct has not left her. She respects sharp minds, blunt words, dry humour, and people who can hold their liquor without becoming sentimental.

Once, Lillith was narrow-minded, cold, alone, and individualistic because that was what her house rewarded. Now, she is still difficult, still sarcastic, still hard to impress, but something in her has shifted into determination. Her past is not something she denies. It is hers to own, to change, and if needed, to scream into the world until something answers.

Her trust is rarely given freely, but once someone has proven useful, loyal, or strangely tolerable, she is more protective than she would ever admit. She finds comfort in odd minds, strange creatures, and people who do not ask her to explain herself too neatly. During her odd jobs, she often lingers at docks, drinking beside sailors, smugglers, travellers, and other half-lost things that smell of salt, smoke, and bad decisions.

She is most herself in the midst of night, beneath a bright sky, following the trajectories of stars as if they might one day draw her a route no map has managed to hold. She has no fixed destination. She only knows she wants to meet new species and races, learn from them, and let them learn from her.

Her favourite food is squid ink pasta.

Ideal:
Freedom. Lillith wants enough coin to leave the delivery business behind, buy a ship, and vanish beyond the mapped world. No matron. No house. No throne. No leash.

In the future, she wishes to become a powerful cleric of Thalassa, carrying the word of her past and future far and wide. She does not seek one holy city, one temple, or one final shore. The road, the sea, and the people she meets along the way are the pilgrimage.

Bond:
Lillith is devoted to Thalassa, the primordial goddess of life, water, and chaos. She sees her faith as a way to kill the past without denying that it shaped her. If her first mother made her into a weapon, Thalassa offers the possibility of being remade into something living.

Flaw:
Lillith often mistakes emotional distance for strength. She assumes every bond will eventually become a transaction, a weakness, or a trap, and may push people away before they can prove otherwise.

Anyone who questions her drowness, or compares her to a Matron Mother, risks setting off something violent and immediate. A blade at the throat. A threat spoken too calmly. A look that makes it clear she has killed the comparison before she has killed the person making it.

Slightly below average drow height (aka still pretty tall), lean, with too-long white hair and deep dark purple-black eyes, Lillith wears a scrambled-together ensemble of cloth, leather, and blade.

Lillith carries a dark iron spear shaped like a harpoon, its haft wrapped in salt-stained leather and threaded with small shells, broken beads, and scraps of sea-worn bone.
fields.appearance:
Above average height, lean, too long white hair, deep dark black eyes, wearing a scrambled together ensamble of cloth leather and blade. 

Lillith carries a dark iron spear shaped like a harpoon, its haft wrapped in salt-stained leather and threaded with small shells, broken beads, and scraps of sea-worn bone.
fields.equipment:
Chain Shirt (Med. Armor)
Amulet (Holy Symbol)
1x Vial of alchemist fire (U can chuck it)
1x Antitoxin
5x healing potions

feats.name: Fey Ancestry
feats.short: Advantage to avoid or end charmed condition
feats.details: Fey Ancestry. You have Advantage on saving throws you make to avoid or end the Charmed condition.

feats.name: Trance
feats.short: You can finish a Long Rest in 4 hours.
feats.details: You don't need to sleep, and magic can't put you to sleep. You can finish a Long Rest in 4 hours if you spend those hours in a trancelike meditation, during which you retain consciousness.

feats.name: Elven Lineage: Drow
feats.short: Darkvision, Dancing Lights, Faerie Fire, Darkness
feats.details:
Lineage: Drow
Level 1: The range of your Darkvision increases to 120 feet. You also know the Dancing Lights cantrip.
Level 3: Faerie Fire
Level 5: Darkness

You are part of a lineage that grants you supernatural abilities. Choose a lineage from the Elven Lineages table. You gain the level 1 benefit of that lineage.

When you reach character levels 3 and 5, you learn a higher-level spell, as shown on the table. You always have that spell prepared. You can cast it once without a spell slot, and you regain the ability to cast it in that way when you finish a Long Rest. You can also cast the spell using any spell slots you have of the appropriate level.

Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma is your spellcasting ability for the spells you cast with this trait (choose the ability when you select the lineage).

feats.name: What's There?
feats.details: You make a deceptive maneuver to distract your foe, allowing you to move away from combat without activating an attack of opportunity: you can disengage from combat as a bonus action for a number of times equal to your proficiency bonus, per long rest.

feats.name: Sorry for the Delay
feats.details: You permanently gain +5 ft in walking, swimming, and climbing movement speed.

spells.name: Dancing Lights
spells.effect: 4 lights within range, 10ft radius, bonus action to move.
spells.details:
Illusion Cantrip (Artificer, Bard, Sorcerer, Wizard)

Casting Time: Action
Range: 120 feet
Components: V, S, M (a bit of phosphorus)
Duration: Concentration, up to 1 minute

You create up to four torch-size lights within range, making them appear as torches, lanterns, or glowing orbs that hover for the duration. Alternatively, you combine the four lights into one glowing Medium form that is vaguely humanlike. Whichever form you choose, each light sheds Dim Light in a 10-foot radius.

As a Bonus Action, you can move the lights up to 60 feet to a space within range. A light must be within 20 feet of another light created by this spell, and a light vanishes if it exceeds the spell’s range.
